body.content: 🟢 ALERT: Staging Database CPU utilization dropped by 45%.
Look at that telemetry. Because the analytical read queries in staging are now natively routed to the isolated schemas, the "noisy neighbor" contention is completely gone.
Query latency has plummeted to 95ms during the simulated peak load test. We have achieved the primary performance target.
@channel 🚨 MILESTONE DELIVERED EARLY 🚨
🎉 🥳 Incredible work, team! The Phase 3 Multi-tenant Database Infrastructure Deployment has been completed, synced, and validated five days ahead of schedule!
🚀 🔥 This is massive. The isolation strategy is working flawlessly. Outstanding execution, James.
🙌 Fantastic news! This gives us incredible momentum going into the microservices extraction.
Because we absorbed the earlier slack time effectively and delivered this milestone early, we are in a prime position to begin Phase 4 immediately. Enjoy the weekend, team.
🎯 Kicking off Phase 4: Microservices Extraction | 🚧 None | ⏭️ Drafting API gateway deployment documentation.
🎯 Extracting the Analytics Engine from the Next.js API folder | 🚧 None | ⏭️ Setting up the standalone Node.js service repository.
🎯 Verifying production WAL data replication | 🚧 None | ⏭️ Refactoring frontend data fetching to hit the new API Gateway.
Marcus, as you extract the Analytics Engine logic, be aware that there is a legacy PDF export module deeply intertwined with the Next.js Server-Side Rendering (SSR) context.
I see it in the codebase. It looks like it utilizes Puppeteer to spin up a headless Chromium browser, render the React component on the server, and print it to PDF.
Yes. It's horrible for memory performance, but our retail clients use it daily for their end-of-month reporting. How are we migrating that specific module to the new Node.js microservice?

Moving a Puppeteer-based headless browser rendering pipeline into a lightweight data microservice is a severe anti-pattern. A backend API should strictly return JSON data, not render visual React components.
But the PDF export *must* work. Can we just rewrite the PDF generator from scratch to use a pure server-side library like `pdfkit` based on the JSON payload?
Rewriting the entire visual reporting engine to use `pdfkit` instead of React components is a massive undertaking. It would take at least three weeks of dedicated development.

@mark-torres @sarah-jenkins We have encountered a scope deviation. The legacy PDF export functionality is tightly coupled to the monolithic UI renderer. Rewriting this entire backend service was not documented in the initial technical discovery or the SOW.
We absolutely cannot drop the PDF exports. It's a core feature tied to several enterprise contracts. You have to include it in the extraction.

Mark, I understand the feature's criticality to the business. However, rewriting a proprietary rendering engine represents significant scope creep. If we absorb this three-week rewrite now, we will miss the final Go-Live date, which directly compromises the SOC 2 security compliance timeline needed for your new enterprise clients.
Elena is right. We cannot jeopardize the overarching SOC 2 compliance timeline just to rewrite a legacy PDF exporter. What is the architectural alternative?

We utilize the Strangler Fig pattern exactly as intended. We extract all the heavy data processing and analytical calculations to the new microservice. We leave the legacy PDF export route *inside* the Next.js monolith for now. When a PDF is requested, the monolith simply acts as a proxy, calling the new microservice for the processed data, rendering the PDF, and returning it.
So the monolith survives, but just as a lightweight proxy for legacy UI rendering?
Exactly. It isolates the performance drain. The heavy lifting is decoupled. We will log the complete deprecation of the Puppeteer module as Technical Debt to be systematically addressed in Q3.
That is a highly acceptable compromise. It keeps the core project on track while preserving the user functionality. Let's proceed with that architecture.

Excellent. I am adding a ticket to the Technical Debt Register for the Q3 roadmap: `Deprecate legacy Next.js Puppeteer PDF export`. This ensures it doesn't get lost, but fiercely protects our current critical path.
User @marcus-vance created a new branch: `feat/analytics-microservice-strangler`
Setting up the proxy middleware in Next.js `middleware.ts` to forward specific `/api/reports` requests to the new external service.
I'll review the Next.js rewrite PR once it's up. Make sure the headers (especially the Auth0 JWT) are being properly forwarded in the proxy configuration.
Creating the new Node/Express API repository in the monorepo workspace. Turborepo is configured to build them concurrently.
Updating the frontend API clients to point to the new gateway URL. Replacing standard fetch wrappers with Axios for better interceptor management.
🎯 Updating Auth0 rules to issue JWTs valid for the new API audience parameter.
Got a CORS error on local dev when the frontend hits the new microservice port. `No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header is present.`
Just pushed a fix to the `cors` middleware options in the Node service. Also added `OPTIONS` to the allowed methods for preflight.
CORS is fixed. Data is flowing through the proxy and populating the frontend charts correctly.

API Gateway routing rules are established. The Next.js frontend is now configured to fetch dynamic visual dashboards via client-side data fetching directly from the microservices, significantly reducing the server rendering load.
User @marcus-vance merged Pull Request #188: `build: finalize turborepo caching`
Checking the CI/CD pipeline run... build time is down from 35 minutes to 8.5 minutes! That is incredible.
Turborepo is fully caching the unchanged workspace packages. Because the monolith is now stripped of its heavy backend logic, the build times have plummeted.
The engineering team definitely owes you that keg, Marcus.
🎯 Managing API Gateway deployment | 🚧 None | ⏭️ Initiating Phase 5 Quality Assurance.
🎯 Analytics Microservice deployed to staging | 🚧 None | ⏭️ Next.js monolith successfully proxying legacy PDF data.
🎯 Validating Auth0 JWT validation at the new API Gateway | 🚧 None | ⏭️ Trickle migration is now at 85%.
🎯 Frontend API integration complete | 🚧 None | ⏭️ Monitoring database connection pooling.
🟢 ALERT: P95 Dashboard Load Time across staging is currently 0.82 seconds.
Sub-second load times! The decoupling worked perfectly. The presentation layer is flying.
By removing the heavy server-side rendering business logic, the Next.js application is acting purely as a presentation layer.
And the API Gateway is handling the JWT validation and routing the requests to the correct Drizzle schema based on the Auth0 context. It is a beautiful, highly resilient architecture.
@channel The core microservices have been successfully extracted and validated in staging. We are officially preparing for Phase 5: Quality Assurance & Production Go-Live!
Please review the QA matrices I've added to the shared drive. We'll start regression testing tomorrow morning.
